# Apple HIG Research

## Source Policy
- Primary sources are official Apple Human Interface Guidelines and official Apple developer guidance.
- Secondary materials are only used to locate Apple pages.
- This research is for implementation guidance only and does not include proprietary design copying or screenshot harvesting.

## Source List
- Apple Human Interface Guidelines (home): https://developer.apple.com/design/human-interface-guidelines/
- HIG: Onboarding: https://developer.apple.com/design/human-interface-guidelines/onboarding?changes=_1_1
- HIG: Tab bars: https://developer.apple.com/design/human-interface-guidelines/tab-bars?changes=la_11
- HIG: Toolbars: https://developer.apple.com/design/human-interface-guidelines/toolbars
- HIG: Alerts: https://developer.apple.com/design/human-interface-guidelines/alerts
- HIG: Motion: https://developer.apple.com/design/Human-Interface-Guidelines/motion
- HIG: Accessibility: https://developer.apple.com/design/human-interface-guidelines/accessibility/
- HIG: Searching: https://developer.apple.com/design/human-interface-guidelines/searching
- HIG: Search fields: https://developer.apple.com/design/human-interface-guidelines/search-fields
- HIG: Text fields: https://developer.apple.com/design/human-interface-guidelines/text-fields/
- HIG: Notifications: https://developer.apple.com/design/human-interface-guidelines/notifications?changes=__6_4&language=objc
- HIG: Managing notifications: https://developer.apple.com/design/human-interface-guidelines/managing-notifications?language=objc
- HIG: Launching: https://developer.apple.com/design/human-interface-guidelines/launching?changes=latest_min_6
- HIG: Privacy: https://developer.apple.com/design/human-interface-guidelines/privacy/
- HIG: In-app purchase: https://developer.apple.com/in-app-purchase/
- HIG: App Store subscriptions: https://developer.apple.com/app-store/subscriptions/
- SwiftUI: List: https://developer.apple.com/documentation/SwiftUI/List
- SwiftUI: NavigationStack: https://developer.apple.com/documentation/swiftui/NavigationStack
- SwiftUI: TabView: https://developer.apple.com/documentation/swiftui/TabView
- SwiftUI: Form: https://developer.apple.com/documentation/swiftui/Form
- User Notifications: requestAuthorization API: https://developer.apple.com/documentation/usernotifications/unusernotificationcenter/requestauthorization%28options%3Acompletionhandler%3A%29
- User Notifications: Asking permission to use notifications: https://developer.apple.com/documentation/usernotifications/asking_permission_to_use_notifications
- App Privacy details (Apple): https://developer.apple.com/app-store/app-privacy-details/

## Applicable HIG Constraints
- Keep navigation and task hierarchy clear, with a small number of top-level destinations. Implication for this app: map "'`home`, `primary_list`, and `profile_settings` into a stable primary structure, not deep chains hidden behind unclear gesture-only controls.
- Use onboarding to remove friction and teach only what is needed at the moment. Implication: `onboarding` should be short, skippable, and immediately show value by reaching task creation quickly.
- Use standard iOS navigation patterns for orientation and consistency. Implication: treat `create_edit`, `active_task`, and `completion` as continuation screens reachable with standard back navigation from the current flow.
- Prefer predictable command placement and system conventions. Implication: place common actions (edit, save, complete, delete) where iOS users expect them in navigation bars/toolbars, and avoid custom icon-only controls that have unclear semantics.
- Respect user interruptions and notification expectations. Implication: avoid excessive reminders, and ensure every reminder for `active_task` has clear utility and user-set context.
- Use motion only for meaningful feedback, not decoration. Implication: keep `completion` transitions minimal and optional; no looping animations.
- Apply accessibility by default. Implication: any custom row/button in `primary_list` and `history_streaks` must expose labels, traits, and state semantics equivalent to standard controls.
- Privacy and trust over retention. Implication: request only the minimum permissions (notifications, tracking only if required), explain why in plain language in `onboarding` and paywall entry points.

## Interaction And Motion Guidance
- Primary navigation should remain discoverable with standard bars, tabs, and back behavior, not custom swipe-only shortcuts.
- Lists must support quick scanning and easy task actions; row actions should remain discoverable without hidden gestures only.
- For list edits in `create_edit`, show validation inline and avoid modal blocking steps unless required.
- For reminders and timed behavior, use standard notification UX and deep-link targets so taps return users to the relevant `active_task` context.
- Keep motion subtle, short, and tied to state change. `completion` can use one lightweight transition/haptic cue for acknowledgment, then immediately return to task context.

## Accessibility Guidance
- Use sufficient contrast, scalable text, and dynamic type-friendly layouts on all screens.
- Ensure custom controls announce full meaning (`Text`, `Progress`, `streak` badges, and toggles) via accessibility labels and values.
- Avoid reliance on color alone in `history_streaks` and `active_task`; pair color with icon/text state.
- Touch targets should be comfortably sized and spaced for fingertip precision.
- Test VoiceOver order from left-to-right, top-to-bottom so each row in `primary_list` and `completion` reads as a complete unit.

## Notification And Interruption Guidance
- Ask for notification authorization only at the point of first meaningful need (e.g., user enables reminders), not during generic onboarding.
- Use grouped scheduling logic so the user is not flooded with repeated alerts.
- Prefer one primary reminder style per channel: morning check-in, due-time reminder, and optional streak feedback.
- Always let users disable reminders and adjust cadence quickly from settings or row actions.
- Surface default-safe notification wording: concise, actionable, and not guilt-inducing.

## Screen Type Implications
- onboarding: one-screen-by-one-screen walkthrough, optional skip, clear “what this app does first,” and immediate path to adding a first task.
- home: provide quick status summary and primary entry point(s) into `primary_list`; avoid dense static cards.
- primary_list: stable list organization, search/filter if scale grows, swipe/edit affordances that are discoverable, and clear empty-state guidance.
- create_edit: short form, clearly grouped sections (title, schedule, reminders), inline validation, and one primary action.
- active_task: focus on single-task state, clear timer or due-time visibility, easy stop/snooze actions, and no extra decoration.
- completion: short success confirmation, optional habit feedback, then clear next-step CTA (repeat, add next task, or return home).
- history_streaks: timeline/history should prioritize readability, month/week trends, and avoid overly dense charts with tiny targets.
- profile_settings: centralize all toggles and account/privacy actions; keep destructive actions gated and clearly labeled.
- paywall_subscription: only show when value is clear, include benefit-focused copy, include restore/retry, and avoid repeated modal interruptions.

## what_to_adapt
- Use SwiftUI canonical containers: `TabView` for main destinations, `NavigationStack` for in-flow depth, `List` for task collections.
- Apply native controls for input: `Form`, labeled text fields, date/time pickers, toggles, and system buttons.
- Use standard permission sheets and in-app explanation patterns before requesting notifications.
- Use iOS-native motion and feedback: minimal `withAnimation` on state transitions, system haptics for completion, and reduced-motion support.
- Add accessibility modifiers at component boundaries for list rows, streak counters, and reminder toggles.
- Keep subscription flow as a dedicated screen/policy block, not as fake notifications or deceptive prompts.

## what_not_to_copy
- Do not replace standard list/table and navigation behavior with custom visual metaphors that reduce clarity.
- Do not force full onboarding completion before core interaction; this blocks first success.
- Do not overuse modal alerts or action sheets for routine task edits.
- Do not ship repetitive reminder schedules, noisy notification text, or non-cancelable notification prompts.
- Do not design paywall screens that mimic system interruptions (auto-popups, forced timers, misleading countdown urgency) or degrade accessibility.
